title: Introductions in the Jefferson Building
summary: Interviewer Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the July 13, 2011 session in the Library of Congress Jefferson Building, welcoming journalists Simeon Booker and Moses Newson for the Civil Rights History Project.

title: Booker From the Afro to Jet
summary: Booker traces his start at the Baltimore Afro-American in 1942 under publisher Carl Murphy, his move to the Cleveland Call & Post, a Nieman Fellowship at Harvard, and his jump to John H. Johnson's new pocket magazine Jet, where he opened the Washington bureau.

title: Newson From the Navy to Memphis
summary: Newson recalls leaving the Navy, being drawn to Black papers like the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, studying at Storer College in West Virginia and Lincoln University's journalism school in Missouri, then joining the Tri-State Defender in Memphis in 1952.

title: Hoxie, Arkansas Opens Its Classrooms
summary: Newson describes covering early school desegregation in Hoxie, Arkansas in 1955, an unusual case where reporters were invited into classrooms and onto the buses, and where integration went smoothly until outside agitators arrived.

title: Clinton, Tennessee and Foley Hill
summary: Newson recounts the 1956 crisis in Clinton, Tennessee, where roughly 200 Black residents lived on Foley Hill, students were jeered walking down to integrate the school, John Kasper and the KKK fired up white residents, a white minister was run out of town, and the governor called out the National Guard.

title: Booker's First Trip to Mississippi
summary: Booker, then based in Chicago, explains that the Emmett Till killing turned his attention to Mississippi, a place wholly new to a reporter raised in Baltimore. He set out with a photographer to investigate how two men had killed the boy over a whistling incident.

title: Mamie Till and the Open Casket
summary: Booker recalls staying up through the night with Mamie Till as the body came in from Mississippi and her insistence that the casket be opened so the world could see what was done to her boy. He remembers a piece of the skull falling off and his photographer replacing it before shooting.

title: Photographer David Jackson
summary: Booker praises Jet photographer David Jackson as a brave and skilled cameraman who handled the murder cases, working in Mississippi at a time when cameras were large and conspicuous and being a Black photographer there was genuinely dangerous.

title: The Till Photo Lifts Jet's Circulation
summary: Booker describes how Jet's circulation took off when it ran the funeral picture, forcing the magazine's first-ever reprint, as the entire Black community grew aware of the need to act on the Till case.

title: A Hateful Climate and George Lee
summary: Newson sets the Mississippi scene after the 1954 school ruling: a hateful climate, unpunished killings, and the murder of George Lee for registering Black voters. He went down for Lee's funeral, where many came to gauge how forceful new NAACP head Roy Wilkins would be.

title: How the Till Killing Unfolded
summary: Newson lays out the facts of the Till case: the whistling incident at the Bryant store, Roy Bryant and his half-brother J.W. Milam being out of town until a young friend mentioned it, and Newson photographing the small whistle-stop hamlet and interviewing Till's grandfather in a cotton field.

title: Finding Witnesses With Medgar Evers
summary: Newson recalls the Tallahatchie County trial roughly three weeks after the killing, being chosen to go out with NAACP teams to round up Black witnesses on Delta plantations, changing into plantation-worker clothes, and riding with driver Medgar Evers to find people who would testify.

title: Jet's Stringer System
summary: Newson explains the stringer network Jet built across rural areas: readers submitted weekly story ideas for pay and became informers and local guides, building a forgotten audience and feeding exclusive items into a weekly column that lit up communities.

title: Segregated Courthouse Press Tables
summary: Newson describes the segregated press arrangements at the Till trial, where the sheriff boasted of no integration and Black reporters had no press table until Congressman Charles C. Diggs Jr. and veteran reporters complained to the judge. He recalls also covering a rape case from a courthouse balcony.
related_events: Charles C. Diggs Jr. observes Till trial 1955

title: Knowing the Little Rock Nine
summary: Booker recalls arriving early in Little Rock in 1957 with a photographer and getting to know the nine students and their parents before the story broke, so he could write who these people were. He also notes how his Washington bureau covered Congress and government to fill the magazine.

title: Ordered Out by the Colonel
summary: Newson describes the National Guard turn at Little Rock after the governor reversed course, his first day being met by a colonel near 16th and Park who said his presence might cause problems, and being escorted back to his car by troops while two men trailed him with threats.

title: The Mob Attack on L. Alex Wilson
summary: Newson recounts hanging around the home of Daisy and L.C. Bates, who led the NAACP and worked closest with the nine students, then walking into a mob with three other reporters. When the children slipped into the school another way, the crowd turned violent and beat L. Alex Wilson of the Tri-State Defender.
related_events: Little Rock mob attack on reporters 1957

title: Why Wilson Would Not Run
summary: Newson explains that L. Alex Wilson refused to run because of an earlier KKK encounter in Leesburg, Florida, after which Wilson vowed never to run again. That vow left him to be beaten badly, and his wife believed the beating hastened his early death.
related_events: Little Rock mob attack on reporters 1957

title: Eisenhower Sends in the Military
summary: Newson argues Little Rock was among the most important civil rights cases because, after the attack and NAACP complaints, President Eisenhower sent in the military, meaning violence could no longer be used openly to block desegregation and reporters and students gained real protection.
related_events: Eisenhower sends troops to Little Rock 1957

title: Elizabeth Eckford Walks Alone
summary: Newson tells the story he most looks back on: Elizabeth Eckford missing word that the students were not to come, arriving alone, turned away by the guard, and surrounded by a hostile white crowd. He interviewed the fifteen-year-old, who said she never thought people could be so cruel.
related_events: Elizabeth Eckford walk to Central High 1957

title: Eisenhower's Silence, Kennedy's Opening
summary: Asked about the mood by the early 1960s, Booker recalls a retirement interview in which Eisenhower acknowledged he had never spoken about civil rights, contrasting that silence with Kennedy, who opened things up to the press and at whose funeral Booker was named a pallbearer.

title: Expectations and the Courts
summary: Newson says people expected more than was delivered, but the administration set a good mood against lawmakers threatening to leave the union, while organizations like the NAACP, Urban League, CORE, and SNCC worked on. He credits judges such as Frank M. Johnson and Earl Warren for knocking down Jim Crow barriers.

title: After Till, a Different Attitude
summary: Newson reflects that the struggle long predated the 1950s and 1960s, citing a Florida family killed during the Christmas season, but contends that the killing of Emmett Till for supposedly whistling at a white woman pushed young people and adults toward greater activism.

title: A Sharecropper's Sacrifice in Church
summary: Newson describes a mass meeting in a Mississippi church near Money, with a Howard University professor speaking while police and KKK photographed attendees to get them fired from plantations. A middle-aged sharecropper, evicted for trying to register, told the crowd to forget him and keep registering to vote.

title: Booker Boards the Freedom Ride
summary: Booker explains he went on the Freedom Ride as an observer with a photographer, riding from Washington toward Atlanta and Anniston, noting the crowds did not distinguish observers from participants. He recalls the bus burning at Anniston, with Newson aboard that bus and himself on the other.

title: A Bureau Chief's Washington Access
summary: Booker reflects on the contacts he built as Washington bureau chief, including the FBI and the administration, and on his role as a Westinghouse radio commentator three times a week, which let him explain Black concerns to white audiences. He notes he is finishing a book about his front-line experiences.

title: Dixie Official Flunks Her Own Test
summary: Newson recalls answering James Farmer's call to join the journey and a New Orleans voting-rights hearing en route, where a registrar testified that Black applicants could not pass her simple tests until Civil Rights Commission lawyers asked her the same questions and she could not answer any of them.

title: Three Rulings Behind the Rides
summary: Newson traces the legal foundation of the Freedom Rides: CORE's 1947 attempt without legal support, a Baltimore woman's 1946 interstate-travel victory, the Rosa Parks situation and the four women represented by Attorney Fred Gray, and Bruce Boynton's case covering bus-station facilities.

title: John Lewis at Rock Hill and King's Warning
summary: Newson watched the riders test facilities in twos and threes, saw John Lewis attacked and bloodied at Rock Hill, South Carolina, and recalls Martin Luther King warning Booker and others that the KKK planned to keep the riders from getting out of Alabama alive, just as James Farmer left after his father's death.

title: The Greyhound Reaches Anniston
summary: Newson, riding the Greyhound, describes arriving at the Anniston, Alabama station to an angry mob that beat on the bus with chains and sticks while the driver locked the door, and a woman returning from a funeral who knelt terrified between the seats, asking why this was happening.

title: The Firebombing and Trooper Cowling
summary: Newson recounts the bus being chased and trapped outside Anniston, plainclothes state troopers aboard, and trooper E.L. Cowling drawing his pistol in the doorway to keep the mob off. A firebomb landed in the empty seat behind him, the bus filled with smoke, and the riders barely escaped before it burned out.

title: Hospital and the Shuttlesworth Caravan
summary: Newson describes losing his camera in the burning bus and later recovering it, the injured riders being taken to an Anniston hospital that wanted them gone, and the young leader standing in for Joe Perkins reaching Rev. Shuttlesworth in Birmingham, who sent a caravan of cars to rescue them.

title: How Close the Danger Came
summary: Reflecting on May 14, 1961, the journalists weigh how close each came to serious injury or death, at Anniston and at the Birmingham terminal where riders were badly beaten, with Booker noting it was simply part of the life that they accepted.

title: Perspectives After the Freedom Ride
summary: Recalling John Lewis's frustrated March on Washington speech, Booker frames his own mission as covering the Black underclass so Jet and Ebony could push education and opportunity, while Newson notes that Black aspirations did not match the administration's view and that reporters glimpsed what J. Edgar Hoover was doing.

title: At the March on Washington
summary: Newson recalls the March on Washington as a historic event he attended with his wife and a colleague's wife, hearing King's speech and witnessing white union members, church leaders, and others coming together to demand change, which cast the day in a completely different light.

title: The Last Interview With King
summary: Booker describes his final extended interview with King during Poor People's Campaign planning, conducted in a car to the Atlanta airport with Andrew Young, King's wife, and Harry Belafonte present. Asked about charges he was failing, King answered that by that logic Jesus Christ failed because He got himself hung on the cross.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. 1968

title: Booker on King's Resolve
summary: Booker remembers King as never appearing disappointed or defeated, always on the go and sustained by a private prayer, and a brave man who carried great stress and rumors of communist associations while plugging away day after day. Asked about Malcolm X and Stokely Carmichael, Booker says he paid them little attention.

title: Abernathy Carries On
summary: Newson says he mostly watched King speak rather than covering him directly, and recalls how King and Ralph Abernathy worked as a team, Abernathy passing the basket while King delivered the speeches. After King's death Abernathy tried to carry on the work in the Alabama Black Belt without King's personality.

title: Covering the Political Conventions
summary: Asked about the 1964 Atlantic City convention and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, Booker recalls covering four conventions, including the Republican gathering in Kansas City where the conservative wing took over the party, but notes he did not cover 1964.

title: Black and White Reporters at Work
summary: Both reflect on relations between Black and white journalists. Booker notes the presses worked to different standards, while Newson recalls being unable to stay at the central press hotels in Memphis, trading help with white colleagues on a professional basis, and keeping his distance from the hostile Mississippi papers.
related_events: Black journalists hired by white papers

title: Television Transforms the Movement
summary: Booker argues that when the white press and TV networks covered the movement, nothing greater could have happened: showing people beaten, hosed, and trampled by horses moved a public that, deep down, valued justice, and accelerated the movement in ways the Black press alone could not.
related_events: Television coverage of the Civil Rights Movement

title: A Suspect Arrest in the Coverage
summary: Booker faults mainstream outlets like the New York Times and Washington Post for their handling of the 2009 Henry Louis Gates Jr. arrest, a disorderly-conduct case he says would not hold up because citizens may question and demand identification from a policeman, yet papers rarely explained why the charges were dropped.

title: Robert Kennedy, Johnson, and Obama
summary: Closing on Robert Kennedy's 1968 assassination, Booker calls him the first official of that rank sensitive to racial matters and willing to act, contrasts him with Lyndon Johnson who had to be boss, and observes that the political structure disintegrated until it was finally rebuilt when Obama came through.
